common_name: Fuyu Persimmon (Non-Astringent Flat Asian Persimmon for Gulf Coast)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter to early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun; Fuyu is non-astringent — unlike Hachiya and most native persimmons, Fuyu can be eaten firm like an apple without puckering astringency, making it the most practical fresh-eating persimmon for home gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fuyu is the most popular non-astringent Asian persimmon for home garden production — producing flat, tomato-shaped, medium-large (4–6 oz), deep-orange fruits that can be eaten while still firm, like an apple, without the intense mouth-puckering astringency that makes other persimmons (including Hachiya) inedible until fully soft. The flat shape, firm texture when ripe, sweet flavor, and brilliant orange color make Fuyu the most practical and versatile fresh-eating persimmon. Fuyu ripens in October–November in zone 9, producing fruit after the summer growing season has ended and providing sweet, colorful fresh fruit at a time when few other locally grown fruits are available. The attractive deciduous tree puts on a spectacular fall display of orange to red fall foliage as the persimmons ripen — a visual combination that is outstanding in the autumn landscape. Fuyu is self-fruitful (does not require a pollinator tree) and produces reliable annual crops on mature trees with minimal pest and disease pressure. Trees are drought-tolerant once established and require minimal fertilizer input — excessive nitrogen encourages vegetative growth at the expense of fruit production. Japanese persimmons (D. kaki) require approximately 100–200 chill hours — readily accumulated in Gulf South winters — making Fuyu well-adapted to zone 9 production.

common_name: Shangri-La Mulberry (Everbearing Compact Gulf South Mulberry)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in winter to early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Shangri-La mulberry is everbearing — fruiting over multiple extended periods rather than in one concentrated flush, providing an extended picking season compared to standard spring-bearing types.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Shangri-La is a compact, everbearing mulberry selection valued for Gulf South gardens where the combination of heat tolerance, low chill requirement, extended fruit production, and moderate size makes it one of the most practical fresh fruit trees for zone 9 home landscapes. The long, cylindrical, dark-purple to black fruits (1.5–2 inches) are sweet, juicy, and excellent for fresh eating, jam, jelly, and freezing — the rich, complex mulberry flavor is one of the finest of any easily grown fruit tree. 'Shangri-La' fruits over an extended season (typically March through June in zone 9, with some additional fall fruiting) rather than in a single compressed flush — providing weeks of fresh mulberry harvest rather than an overwhelming glut. The tree's smaller mature size (8–12 feet compared to 20–30 feet for seedling mulberries) makes it manageable in typical home landscapes and allows netting to protect fruit from birds, which are very competitive with human harvesters for mulberry fruit. Mulberries in the Gulf South require very few chill hours (100–200 hours) and fruit reliably in warm winters that defeat other tree fruits. Established trees are highly drought-tolerant and require minimal care — the most significant maintenance is the control of surface root spread and the cleanup of fallen fruit on paved surfaces.

common_name: Ice Cream Banana (Blue Java Cold-Hardy Vanilla-Flavored Banana)

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Blue Java tolerates frost to approximately 20°F (-7°C) — the pseudostem may be killed but the rhizome survives most zone 9 winters and regenerates new shoots in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost; Blue Java (Ice Cream banana) is one of the most cold-hardy banana cultivars available for Gulf South gardens — tolerating frost temperatures to approximately 20°F, allowing the rhizome to survive most zone 9 winters even when the pseudostem is frozen to the ground and regenerate productive new shoots in spring. The variety name 'Blue Java' refers to the distinctive blue-green color of the unripe fruit bunches; the 'Ice Cream' name describes the rich, creamy, vanilla-like flavor of the ripe fruit — a flavor profile distinctly different from the standard Cavendish banana of commerce and more complex, with smooth, creamy, slightly vanilla-custard characteristics that justify the evocative name. The medium-length (6–8 inch), pale-yellow ripe fruits have a soft, creamy texture that reinforces the ice cream comparison. In zone 9, Blue Java typically requires the full warm season (May–October) for pseudostem development, and established plants with multiple offsets may fruit in warm years with protection through mild winters. Even when pseudostems are killed by frost, the vigorous rhizome system regenerates multiple new shoots in spring, making Blue Java a reliable ornamental tropical even in years when fruit production is not achieved. Heavy mulching around the base protects the rhizome during cold snaps.

common_name: Orinoco Banana (Very Cold-Hardy Cooking and Eating Gulf South Banana)

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Orinoco is one of the most cold-tolerant banana cultivars — the rhizome survives temperatures into the high teens (°F) and regenerates reliably from the roots in spring in zone 8b–9.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost; Orinoco is among the most cold-hardy banana cultivars for Gulf South gardens — a large, vigorous variety with remarkable rhizome hardiness that allows reliable regrowth from the roots even after severe freezes that kill the pseudostem entirely. The cold hardiness is Orinoco's primary practical advantage over most other banana cultivars in zone 8b–9: while the aboveground pseudostem is typically killed in hard freezes below 25–28°F, the underground rhizome system survives temperatures into the upper teens (°F), regenerating aggressively in spring and frequently reaching full pseudostem height again within a single growing season. Orinoco produces medium-large, thick-skinned, angular-ribbed fruits that are excellent for cooking when green (plantain-like, with a slightly starchy, mild flavor that takes on seasonings well in frying, baking, and sautéing) and sweet for fresh eating when fully ripe and yellow to black. The thick skin provides good shelf life and handling resistance. The very large, bold tropical foliage (8–12 foot pseudostems with 4–6 foot leaves in Gulf South conditions) creates dramatic tropical landscape impact even in years when fruit production is not the priority. Orinoco has been grown in Gulf South gardens for generations and is particularly favored in coastal Louisiana where its heat and humidity tolerance are well-matched to the climate.

common_name: Golden Nugget Loquat (Large-Fruited Sweet Named Loquat Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container trees in fall through spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Golden Nugget is self-fruitful and does not require a second tree for fruit production — a significant practical advantage for small home gardens where space for two large trees may not be available.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Golden Nugget is one of the best-fruiting named loquat cultivars for Gulf South gardens — producing larger, sweeter, better-quality fruits than seedling loquats with the same outstanding adaptability to Gulf Coast conditions that makes loquat one of the most reliable subtropical fruit trees for zone 8b–9. The round to oval, deep golden-orange fruits are substantially larger than typical seedling loquat (1.5–2 inches diameter compared to 1–1.5 inches for seedlings) and have excellent sweet flavor with a mild, tropical quality. Loquats bloom in fall and winter (November–January in zone 9) and ripen fruit in early spring (February–March) — providing fresh tree fruit at a time when virtually no other locally grown fruit is available. The winter flowering is the primary limitation of loquat production in zone 9: the flowers and young fruit are frost-sensitive, and a hard freeze during the fruit development period can eliminate the entire crop. In years without hard freezes, Golden Nugget produces heavy crops of high-quality fruit. The evergreen tree is attractive year-round with large, bold, dark-green leaves and is suitable for use as a landscape tree as well as a fruit producer. Loquat tolerates a wide range of soils, partial shade when necessary, and is highly adapted to Gulf Coast humidity and heat.
